filename: Press technique common problems guide PTC.pdf
source: Press technique common problems guide PTC.pdf
strategy: single chunk (file too short to split)

pages.text:
Wrist position
Your elbow should stay directly, vertically below the wrist at all times. Don't let your wrist
slack over backwards. This will reduce force transfer from your torso and arms to the
bar, creating an 'energy leak'.

Left: slacked wrist. Right: properly aligned wrist.
Grab the bar with your thumb first and only then wrap your fingers around the bar. The
weight of the bar should be mostly supported by your thumb, not your fingers.

How to grab the bar.

Squeeze the bar hard during the entire set, especially during the descent. The bar
should be like an extension of your hands to fully transfer all the force you produce into
upward movement.
Proper wrist alignment during the overhead press.

The correct bottom position of the overhead press. Note how in the bottom position the
wrists are still vertically aligned.

Overhead press bar path

The bar path should be as vertical as possible, though it will naturally have to move in a
slight J-curve just as during the bench press. Your head should move out of the way of
the bar, not the other way around. Lean back in the bottom position far enough to lower
the bar to your clavicle without letting your wrists fold backwards (see the grip section
above). The necessity for hip movement for an optimal bar path makes it impossible to
perform an optimal overhead press while sitting: standing overhead presses achieve
higher muscle activity in practically all target muscles than seated overhead presses.

The correct way to press a barbell overhead.

Bad technique. The wrists are bent backwards, the elbows are not directly below the
wrists, there is no backward lean in the bottom position and his head is in the way of a
vertical bar path.

Correct overhead press technique.

During dumbbell overhead presses, the overall technique is similar, but because you
don't need to move your head out of the way of the dumbbells, you shouldn't need as
much hip movement, just enough to get your elbows all the way down for maximum
range of motion while keeping your forearms vertical.

full_text:
--- Page 1 ---
PRESS TECHNIQUE
COMMON PROBLEMS


--- Page 2 ---
Wrist position
Your elbow should stay directly, vertically below the wrist at all times. Don't let your wrist
slack over backwards. This will reduce force transfer from your torso and arms to the
bar, creating an 'energy leak'.

Left: slacked wrist. Right: properly aligned wrist.
Grab the bar with your thumb first and only then wrap your fingers around the bar. The
weight of the bar should be mostly supported by your thumb, not your fingers.


--- Page 3 ---



--- Page 4 ---
How to grab the bar.

Squeeze the bar hard during the entire set, especially during the descent. The bar
should be like an extension of your hands to fully transfer all the force you produce into
upward movement.


--- Page 5 ---
Proper wrist alignment during the overhead press.


--- Page 6 ---
The correct bottom position of the overhead press. Note how in the bottom position the
wrists are still vertically aligned.


--- Page 7 ---
Overhead press bar path

The bar path should be as vertical as possible, though it will naturally have to move in a
slight J-curve just as during the bench press. Your head should move out of the way of
the bar, not the other way around. Lean back in the bottom position far enough to lower
the bar to your clavicle without letting your wrists fold backwards (see the grip section
above). The necessity for hip movement for an optimal bar path makes it impossible to
perform an optimal overhead press while sitting: standing overhead presses achieve
higher muscle activity in practically all target muscles than seated overhead presses.

The correct way to press a barbell overhead.


--- Page 8 ---
Bad technique. The wrists are bent backwards, the elbows are not directly below the
wrists, there is no backward lean in the bottom position and his head is in the way of a
vertical bar path.

Correct overhead press technique.


--- Page 9 ---
During dumbbell overhead presses, the overall technique is similar, but because you
don't need to move your head out of the way of the dumbbells, you shouldn't need as
much hip movement, just enough to get your elbows all the way down for maximum
range of motion while keeping your forearms vertical.
